title: Iodine on Linen — Deep Penetration Reduction
stainChemistry: Iodine on linen penetrates more deeply than on cotton because linen fibers are larger in diameter but more porous due to their crystalline structure. The I₂ molecules form charge-transfer complexes with cellulose hydroxyl groups throughout the fiber depth, not just on the surface. Linen's greater porosity means the stain travels faster and bonds more extensively through the fiber structure.
whyThisWorks: Sodium thiosulfate reduces I₂ to iodide (I⁻) as in cotton treatment, but the extended dwell time is critical because the thiosulfate solution must penetrate the full depth of linen's larger fiber structure. Extended contact time allows the reducing agent to reach iodine molecules deep within the fiber. Hydrogen peroxide follow-up oxidizes any residual brown compounds formed during the reduction process.

spottingProtocol.equipment: spotting brush, white cotton terry pad, softener brush
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply dry sodium thiosulfate powder to the iodine stain on the dry side. Work gently into the fibers with a softener brush. Allow the powder to sit and absorb surface iodine. Linen's porosity will draw the powder deeper into the fiber structure than cotton would.

spottingProtocol.agent: sodium thiosulfate solution (1.5 oz hypo per 16 oz water, tepid)
spottingProtocol.equipment: white terry pad, spotting bottle, gentle pressure
spottingProtocol.instruction: Sponge the thiosulfate solution into the stain from the back side. Use more solution and longer dwell time than cotton — linen's depth requires extended contact. Sponge, let sit 2-3 minutes, repeat 4-5 times total. Keep the area moist throughout. The blue-black color will fade as you progress.

Sponge with hydrogen peroxide 3% to remove any brown residual stain from oxidation products. Work gently to avoid over-bleaching. Linen is more robust than cotton but can still yellow if peroxide is too concentrated or left too long.

spottingProtocol.equipment: white terry pad, spotting bottle, towel
spottingProtocol.instruction: Rinse thoroughly with distilled water multiple times until all chemical residue is gone. Linen's porosity means residual chemicals can remain longer. Use plenty of fresh water and replace the under-pad between rinses.
Final sponge with acetic acid solution (white vinegar 1:3 with water) to neutralize and restore pH balance. This prevents post-spotting yellowing, which linen is susceptible to.

escalation: If the brown iodine stain persists after two full treatment cycles, stop. The iodine may have formed insoluble compounds or oxidized irreversibly. Professional dry-clean solvent bath with nonionic detergent is the next step, but success is not guaranteed. Very old iodine stains on linen are often permanent.
commonMistakes: Insufficient dwell time — rushing the sodium thiosulfate treatment means the solution doesn't reach deep into linen's fiber structure.
Using hot water — heat can set the stain and accelerate oxidation.
Assuming all brown stains are residual iodine — some may be fiber damage.
scienceNote: Linen's larger, more porous fiber structure explains why spotting protocols must be adjusted by surface. The same chemistry (reduction of I₂ to I⁻) works, but the kinetics change. This is why the Eisen method requires understanding both the chemical mechanism AND the fiber's physical properties.
safety: All reagents are non-toxic. Sodium thiosulfate and hydrogen peroxide 3% are safe on linen. Standard spotting gloves recommended. Test hydrogen peroxide on an inconspicuous area to check for color fastness.
materialWarnings: Linen is chemically stable with all reagents.
However, linen can yellow if exposed to heat or excessive peroxide.
Do not heat-dry the garment immediately after spotting — air dry flat or on a hanger.
Some old linen may have sizing that could be affected by extended water exposure.
callPro: If the garment is valuable linen (antique, heirloom, or fine quality), professional treatment is safer. Linen is durable but can lose sheen and color if over-treated.
customerExplanation: Iodine penetrates deeply into linen's porous structure. We used sodium thiosulfate solution with extended contact time to reach the stain throughout the fiber. After reduction, hydrogen peroxide removed residual brown oxidation products. The garment has been fully rinsed and neutralized.
homeSolutions: Soak the stained area in cold milk for 15-20 minutes (the protein in milk can bind some iodine), then rinse with cold water. More effective on linen than cotton due to fiber depth.
Apply a paste of baking soda and water, let sit for 10 minutes, brush off and rinse. The alkalinity may help slightly.
Soak in white vinegar solution (1 part vinegar to 3 parts water) for 30 minutes, then rinse thoroughly. The acid can slightly help dissolve iodine compounds.

meta.sourceRef: Expert Garment Restoration & Stain Removal Tips

products.name: Sodium Thiosulfate (Hypo Crystals)
products.note: Essential for iodine reduction. Linen stains require larger quantities and longer contact time. 1 lb container lasts for hundreds of treatments.

products.name: Hydrogen Peroxide 3% USP
products.note: For residual brown stain removal and final bleaching. Standard strength is safe on linen.
